1:
‘There be some… which shall not taste of death, till they have seen the kingdom of God come with power.’ The witnesses agree this saying belongs with the preceding discourse, but divide widely over what coming is meant.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the options. Robertson notes Mark’s perfect participle (‘come,’ already arrived) ‘with power,’ against Matthew’s ‘the Son of man coming’ (Matthew 16:28), and that the second coming ‘did not take place while some of those standing there still lived.’ Lightfoot refers it to ‘the coming of Christ in his vengeance and power to destroy’ Jerusalem; Ellicott reads ‘until they shall see the kingdom of God’ as the kingdom ‘actually come in its completeness.’

The fathers and the Reformed largely refer it to the Transfiguration that immediately follows. Pseudo-Jerome holds that ‘after the cross… the glory of the resurrection is shewn, that they… might not fear the shame of the cross’; the chapter division (Ellicott notes) was probably made to connect the saying with the Transfiguration as its fulfilment.

The Puritan Henry and Wesleyan Wesley take it of the kingdom set up in power: Henry of the Messiah’s reign established by the destruction of the Jewish polity and the conquest of Gentile idolatry; Wesley that it ‘began to do’ so at Pentecost, ‘when three thousand were converted… at once’ (Acts 2:41). The shared, dominant note is that some then living would see the kingdom come with power; whether in the Transfiguration, Pentecost, or the fall of Jerusalem, all agree it was a coming within that generation, not the final advent. Chrysostom and the other fathers, taking the saying of the Transfiguration, hold that the chosen three were granted a glimpse of the kingdom’s glory before the cross; Henry adds that it came ‘with power’ both when vengeance fell on the Jews and when the gospel conquered the idolatry of the Gentile world. The witnesses, however they locate the coming, agree it fell within that generation, a pledge that the kingdom Jesus proclaimed would not fail but be established in manifest power. The saying thus stands as a pledge that the kingdom Jesus proclaimed would surely come, and come within the lifetime of some who heard him.
2:
‘After six days Jesus taketh Peter, James, and John… and he was transfigured before them.’ The witnesses agree on the Transfiguration as a foretaste of glory, and discuss its time, place, and nature.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the details. Robertson notes the three taken ‘by themselves’ (a word only in Mark), and that Luke adds the motive ‘to pray’ (Luke 9:28). Clarke conjectures the ‘high mountain’ was near Caesarea Philippi rather than Tabor; Lightfoot, at length, doubts the traditional Tabor since Christ was last in the coasts of Caesarea Philippi (Mark 8:27).

The fathers expound its meaning. Chrysostom explains the six days’ interval filled the disciples with ‘eager desire,’ and that Moses and Elijah were brought to show ‘the difference between the Lord and his servants’ and to refute the charge of transgressing the Law. Theophylact and Pseudo-Chrysostom insist the transfiguration was ‘not the change of his features, but… a certain ineffable brightness’ added; Bede that he ‘shewed forth the glory of… our future resurrection.’

The Wesleyan Wesley links the ‘transfigured’ to the ‘form of God’ and ‘form of a servant’ (Philippians 2:6-7), the divine rays making the glorious change. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, taking the three chosen apostles up the mountain, was transfigured before them — not altered in substance but irradiated with an unveiled, ineffable brightness, a foretaste of his resurrection glory and of the glory awaiting his elect, given to brace them against the coming shame of the cross. Chrysostom adds that Moses and Elijah were brought to show ‘the difference between the Lord and his servants’ and to refute the charge that he transgressed the Law; the witnesses agree the Transfiguration was no change of substance but the breaking forth of an indwelling glory, a foretaste of the resurrection given to steady the three against the coming shame of the cross. Bede adds that he showed forth the glory of our future resurrection, such as he will appear to all his elect after the judgment. Lightfoot, at length, doubts the traditional Tabor, since Christ was last in the coasts of Caesarea Philippi and seems not to have changed his place before this story.
3:
‘His raiment became shining, exceeding white… so as no fuller on earth can white them.’ The few voices note the dazzling whiteness, Mark’s homely comparison peculiar to him.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the brightness. Robertson notes Matthew has ‘white as the light’ (Matthew 17:2) and Luke ‘dazzling’ like lightning, while Mark’s ‘no fuller on earth can whiten them’ is the vivid, homely touch; the snow-capped Hermon may have been visible that very night. Ellicott notes the comparison with cloth fresh from the bleacher’s hands is ‘in its homely vividness, peculiar to St. Mark,’ and that ‘as snow’ is wanting in the best manuscripts. Barnes corrects ‘fuller’ to ‘scourer’ — one who whitens cloth.

Gill says the rays of glory darting from his body made the clothes ‘as bright as the light of the sun at noonday.’ Wesley calls it a whiteness ‘such as could not be equalled either by nature or art.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the glory that irradiated Christ’s person overflowed even to his garments, which shone with a whiteness no earthly art could rival. The grammatical commentators dwell on Mark’s characteristic homeliness — the bleacher who could never produce such whiteness — as a touch of the eyewitness; and all see in the shining raiment the outward sign of an inward and divine glory, the brightness of the Godhead breaking, for a moment, through the veil of the flesh. Gill says the rays of glory darting from his body made the clothes ‘as bright as the light of the sun at noonday,’ and Wesley that the whiteness could be ‘equalled neither by nature nor art’; the witnesses see in the shining raiment the outward sign of an inward and divine glory, the brightness of the Godhead breaking, for a moment, through the veil of the flesh. The grammatical commentators dwell on Mark’s homely comparison — the bleacher who could never match such whiteness — as a touch of the eyewitness, drawn perhaps from Peter himself. Robertson notes Matthew has ‘white as the light’ and Luke ‘dazzling’ like lightning, the snow-capped Hermon perhaps visible on that very night.
4:
‘And there appeared unto them Elias with Moses: and they were talking with Jesus.’ The few voices note the two great representatives of the Law and the Prophets in converse with Christ.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that Matthew and Luke have ‘Moses and Elijah,’ Mark the other order, and that both ‘were prophets and both dealt with law… both had mysterious deaths.’ Wesley remarks tersely on the pair: ‘Elijah, whom they expected; Moses, whom they did not.’ Gill simply notes their appearance.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical passage on the descent of the Spirit at Pentecost, and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Moses and Elijah, the great heads of the Law and the Prophets, appeared in glory and spoke with the transfigured Christ. The grammatical commentators note the fitness of the two — both prophets, both lawgivers in their measure, both attended by mysterious deaths — here doing homage to the Lord whom Law and Prophets alike foretold. Their presence, the witnesses agree, both attested that Jesus was no transgressor of the Law but its fulfilment, and marked the difference between the servants and the Son; for, as Luke records, they spoke with him of the ‘decease’ he should accomplish at Jerusalem, so that the very glory of the mount pointed forward to the cross. Robertson notes the fitness of the pair, both prophets and both attended by mysterious deaths, here doing homage to the Lord whom Law and Prophets foretold; the witnesses agree their presence attested that Jesus was no transgressor of the Law but its fulfilment, and that, as Luke records, they spoke with him of the decease he should accomplish at Jerusalem, so the glory of the mount pointed forward to the cross. Wesley remarks tersely on the pair, ‘Elijah, whom they expected; Moses, whom they did not,’ the two great heads of Law and Prophets together bearing witness to Christ. Robertson observes that Matthew and Luke have ‘Moses and Elijah,’ Mark the other order, both being prophets who dealt with the Law and both attended by mysterious deaths.
5:
‘Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles.’ The three brief voices note Peter’s impulsive wish to prolong the vision.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that Mark, ‘after his manner, gives the Hebrew Rabbi’ (‘Master’) where Matthew has ‘Lord’ and Luke ‘Master.’ Barnes points to the joy of the holy mount (compare Psalms 84:10). Gill notes Peter addressed Christ ‘as being more familiar with him… the principal person.’

With only three brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is clear and agreed. Peter, overwhelmed by the glory and the company of the heavenly visitants, blurts out that it is good to be there and proposes to build three booths — one for Jesus, one for Moses, one for Elijah — as though to detain the vision and dwell upon the mount.

The shared, dominant note is the mingling of true devotion and human frailty in Peter’s words. His sense that it was ‘good to be here’ was right — the foretaste of glory is indeed the soul’s delight — yet his desire to stay betrayed both ignorance and a shrinking from the cross that lay ahead. The grammatical commentators note Mark’s preservation of the Aramaic ‘Rabbi,’ a mark of the eyewitness; and the older writers see in Peter’s proposal a well-meant but mistaken wish to grasp the glory before its time, forgetting that the way to that abiding glory ran first through Gethsemane and Golgotha. Barnes points to the joy of the holy mount, and Gill that Peter, as the foremost, spoke for the three; the witnesses see in his words a mingling of true devotion and human frailty — his sense that it was good to be there was right, yet his wish to stay betrayed a shrinking from the cross, forgetting that the way to abiding glory ran first through Gethsemane and Golgotha. Ellicott notes that Mark, after his manner, gives the Hebrew ‘Rabbi’ where Matthew has ‘Lord,’ a mark of the eyewitness preserved through Peter. Barnes points to the joy of the holy mount, where it is good to be in the presence of God and his glory, though Peter knew not the cost that lay ahead.
6:
‘For he wist not what to say; for they were sore afraid.’ The few voices note Peter’s bewilderment and the disciples’ awe.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the deliberative subjunctive (‘what to answer’), and that Luke excuses Peter as ‘not knowing what he said’; perhaps Peter ‘felt embarrassed at having been asleep’ (Luke 9:32), and the feast of tabernacles was near. He notes the disciples had not heard the talk of Moses and Elijah about Christ’s ‘decease,’ the comfort Jesus drew from it. Barnes says Peter ‘was desirous of saying something, and knew not what would be proper.’

Gill explains he ‘did not know what he should say… in such circumstances, and before such persons.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Peter spoke not from understanding but from fright, ‘not knowing what he said.’ The grammatical commentators note that the awe of the disciples — ‘sore afraid’ — explains the inappropriateness of his proposal; overwhelmed by the glory and the heavenly visitants, he grasped at words to fill the silence. The witnesses see in Peter’s confusion the proper effect of the divine presence upon frail men, and a foil to the calm majesty of the Christ, who alone among them was at home in that glory and who knew, as they did not, that its path lay through the cross of which Moses and Elijah had just been speaking. Robertson adds that the disciples had not heard the talk of Moses and Elijah about Christ’s ‘decease,’ and little knew the comfort he drew from it; the witnesses see in Peter’s confusion the proper effect of the divine presence upon frail men, and a foil to the calm majesty of the Christ, who alone was at home in that glory and knew its path lay through the cross. Barnes notes that Peter ‘was desirous of saying something, and knew not what would be proper,’ overwhelmed by the glory and the heavenly visitants. Robertson notes the deliberative subjunctive, ‘what to answer,’ and that Luke excuses Peter as ‘not knowing what he said,’ perhaps embarrassed at having slept.
7:
‘This is my beloved Son: hear him.’ The witnesses agree the Father’s voice from the cloud crowns the vision, setting Christ above Moses and Elijah.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the details. Ellicott observes Mark omits ‘in whom I am well pleased’ (kept in Matthew 17:5). Barnes ties ‘hear him’ to the prophecy of the prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15). Gill notes the cloud overshadowed Jesus, Moses, and Elijah, and the disciples entering it.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads the bright cloud as ‘such a cloud of glory’ as accompanied Israel in the wilderness, departed at Moses’s death and now returning to honour Christ, ‘the great Prophet of the Church’; ‘hear ye him’ — ‘even preferably to Moses and Elijah.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem turns the verse to a polemic against the Jews, urging worship of the Son whom the Father owns. The shared, dominant note is that the Father’s own voice attests the deity and supremacy of Christ: he is the beloved Son, and the command ‘hear him’ sets his word above the Law and the Prophets, represented by Moses and Elijah, who now disappear. The witnesses see in the overshadowing cloud the glory of the divine presence, and in the voice the divine seal upon all that Jesus had taught — including, against the disciples’ resistance, his coming death; so that the mount’s highest word is not Peter’s proposal but the Father’s charge to listen to the Son. Barnes ties ‘hear him’ to the prophecy of the prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15), and Gill notes the cloud overshadowed Jesus, Moses, and Elijah, the disciples entering it; the witnesses agree the Father’s voice set the Son’s word above the Law and the Prophets, and sealed all he had taught, including the coming death the disciples resisted, so the mount’s highest word is the charge to hear the beloved Son. Ellicott observes that Mark omits ‘in whom I am well pleased,’ kept in Matthew 17:5, yet the Father’s testimony to the Son’s deity stands undiminished. Wesley reads the bright cloud as the cloud of glory that accompanied Israel in the wilderness, departed at Moses’s death and now returning to honour Christ.
8:
‘And suddenly, when they had looked round about, they saw no man any more, save Jesus only.’ The two voices note the sudden vanishing of the vision, leaving Jesus alone.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Mark is ‘more graphic’ than Matthew’s ‘lifting up their eyes’ (Matthew 17:8): ‘the sudden glance around on the mountain side when the cloud with Moses and Elijah was gone,’ and that they ‘were sore afraid’ before Jesus touched them. Gill notes they looked round ‘to see if they could observe any other object’ and found only Jesus.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. As suddenly as the vision had come, it passed: the cloud lifted, the heavenly visitants were gone, and the disciples, glancing about, saw no one but Jesus. The dazzling company of Moses and Elijah, the bright cloud, the Father’s voice — all withdrew, and the Son remained alone with his own.

The shared, dominant note, drawn out by the older writers and implicit in the text, is that ‘Jesus only’ is the abiding word of the mount. The Law and the Prophets, having borne their witness, give place to the Son; the glory fades, but Christ remains. The witnesses see in the scene both a comfort — that when every other support is withdrawn, the disciple still has Christ — and a charge answering the Father’s voice: having been told to hear the beloved Son, they are left with him alone, to follow him down from the mount of glory toward the valley of need and the cross beyond. Gill notes they looked round ‘to see if they could observe any other object’ and found only Jesus; the witnesses see in ‘Jesus only’ the abiding word of the mount — the Law and the Prophets give place to the Son, the glory fades but Christ remains — a comfort that when every other support is withdrawn the disciple still has Christ, and a charge to follow him down toward the valley and the cross. Gould and the grammatical commentators note they ‘were sore afraid’ before Jesus touched them, the vision’s sudden withdrawal leaving the Son alone with his own.
9:
‘He charged them that they should tell no man… till the Son of man were risen from the dead.’ The witnesses agree the silence was enjoined till the resurrection, and the fathers tie it to the disciples’ puzzle over the Elijah prophecy.

The fathers expound the charge. Origen and Chrysostom hold the silence was enjoined so that the glory not be published before the Passion and resurrection; Chrysostom adds that mentioning his Passion ‘implies the cause’ of the silence. Theophylact says it was lest men ‘be offended, hearing such glorious things of him whom they were about to see crucified.’ Pseudo-Jerome reads the peculiar Markan clause — that when ‘death shall have been swallowed up in victory, we shall have no memory for the former things.’

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Matthew has ‘until’ and Mark ‘save when’ the Son should rise, and that it was ‘a high and holy secret experience’ for the chosen three. Gill notes the descent from the mountain.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here treats the great tribulation, tangential to the verse. The shared, dominant note is that Christ sealed the vision until his resurrection, both because its glory could not be rightly understood before the cross and because only the risen Lord would make it credible. The witnesses see in the charge a double wisdom: it guarded the disciples from publishing what would only offend a people about to see him crucified, and it bound the meaning of the Transfiguration to the death and rising it foreshadowed — the glory of the mount being the pledge of the glory beyond the grave. Robertson notes Mark’s ‘save when’ the Son should rise, where Matthew has ‘until,’ and that it was ‘a high and holy secret experience’ for the three; the witnesses agree the charge bound the meaning of the Transfiguration to the death and rising it foreshadowed, guarding the disciples from publishing what would only offend a people about to see him crucified. Theophylact adds it was lest men be offended hearing such glory of one about to be crucified, the glory of the mount being the pledge of the glory beyond the grave.
10:
‘And they kept that saying… questioning one with another what the rising from the dead should mean.’ The few voices note the disciples’ puzzlement over the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the perplexity. Robertson notes they ‘kept the saying to themselves’ as charged, but now noticed his allusion to rising from the dead which had escaped them before (Mark 8:31). Ellicott calls it a ‘characteristic touch, analysing the mental condition of the disciples.’ Clarke notes the clause has no parallel in Matthew or Luke. Gill explains they observed and laid hold of what he said about rising from the dead.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the three obeyed the charge of silence, yet among themselves debated what ‘the rising from the dead’ could mean. They believed in a general resurrection at the last day, but a rising of the Messiah, so soon and so particular, baffled them, bound as they were to the expectation of a Christ who should abide for ever.

The shared, dominant note is the persistence of the disciples’ incomprehension. Even after the glory of the mount and the express words of their Lord, they could not fit his death and resurrection into their picture of the Messiah’s kingdom. The witnesses see in this, as Ellicott observes, one of Mark’s honest analyses of the disciples’ slowness; and a comfort to all who find the deep things of God hard to grasp, since the Lord bore patiently with followers who kept his word even while they could not yet understand it. Clarke notes the clause has no parallel in Matthew or Luke, and Gill that they laid hold of what he said about rising from the dead; the witnesses see in their persistent incomprehension — even after the mount — the grip of the expectation of a Messiah who should abide for ever, and a comfort to all who find the deep things of God hard to grasp, with whom the Lord bore patiently. Ellicott calls it a characteristic Markan touch, analysing the mental condition of the disciples, who could not yet fit a dying and rising Messiah into their hopes.
11:
‘Why say the scribes that Elias must first come?’ The two voices note the disciples’ question, prompted by seeing Elijah on the mount.

The grammatical-historical Barnes refers to the parallel in Matthew 17:10-13 and the prophecy of Elijah’s coming (Malachi 4:5). Gill notes the question arose ‘being put in mind’ by seeing Elijah, or by Christ’s words about his resurrection.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Having just seen Elijah in glory and then vanish, and having heard their Master speak of rising from the dead, the disciples raise the scribes’ objection: if Messiah is come, why must Elijah come first, as Malachi foretold and the scribes taught?

The shared, dominant note is that the disciples were wrestling, through the scribes’ teaching, with how the Transfiguration and the Messiahship of Jesus fit the prophecy of Elijah’s forerunning. Their question, drawn from Malachi 4:5, sets up Christ’s answer in the verses that follow — that Elijah is indeed come, in John the Baptist, who fulfilled the prophecy and suffered as it was written. The witnesses see in the exchange both the disciples’ honest effort to reconcile what they had seen with what they had been taught, and Christ’s patient correction of the scribal expectation, which looked for a literal return of the Tishbite rather than the forerunner who came in his spirit and power. Gill notes the question arose ‘being put in mind’ by seeing Elijah, or by Christ’s words about his resurrection; the witnesses agree the disciples were wrestling, through the scribes’ teaching, with how the Transfiguration and the Messiahship of Jesus fit the prophecy of Elijah’s forerunning, a question that sets up Christ’s answer that Elijah is indeed come in John the Baptist. Their question, drawn from Malachi 4:5, thus sets up Christ’s patient correction of the scribal expectation, which looked for a literal return of the Tishbite. Barnes refers to the parallel in Matthew 17:10-13 and the prophecy of Elijah’s coming, which the disciples were striving to reconcile with what they had seen.
12:
‘Elias verily cometh first, and restoreth all things; and how it is written of the Son of man, that he must suffer.’ The witnesses agree Christ affirms the scribes’ point while correcting its application.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘restoreth all things’ is Christ’s description of the Baptist as the promised Elijah and forerunner (Malachi 4:5-6), and that he ‘patiently enlightens his dull pupils.’ Clarke and Wesley note the joining of the two clauses: Elijah’s coming is ‘not inconsistent with my suffering’ — he is come, yet the Son of man must still suffer, as it is written (Isaiah 53:3).

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem here is a Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse. Gill notes Christ allowed the scribes’ observation was right ‘when rightly understood.’

The shared, dominant note is that Christ confirms the prophecy of Elijah’s coming yet binds it to the Scripture of the suffering Son of man. He does not deny that Elijah comes and restores, but presses the second, harder truth that the Messiah himself must be set at nought and suffer many things, as the prophets foretold. The witnesses see in the answer both a correction of the scribes — who used the Elijah prophecy to deny a suffering Christ — and a patient instruction of the disciples, that the glory they had seen on the mount and the forerunner they looked for were no contradiction of the cross, but were written alongside it in the same Scriptures. Clarke notes the better reading ‘as also it is written,’ and Wesley that the verse both answers their question about Elijah and refutes their error about the Messiah’s continuing for ever; the witnesses agree Christ confirms the prophecy of Elijah’s coming yet binds it to the Scripture of the suffering Son of man, correcting the scribes who used the one to deny the other. The witnesses agree the glory of the mount and the forerunner they looked for were no contradiction of the cross, but were written alongside it in the same Scriptures. Clarke notes the better reading ‘as also it is written,’ and Gill that Christ allowed the scribes’ point was right when rightly understood.
13:
‘But I say unto you, That Elias is indeed come… as it is written of him.’ The few voices agree Christ identifies the Baptist as the Elijah of prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators make the identification. Barnes refers to Matthew 11:14 and Luke 1:17, where John comes ‘in the spirit and power of Elias.’ Ellicott notes the clause ‘as it is written of him’ is peculiar to Mark, pointing both to Malachi’s prophecy and to the parallel between John’s career and the Tishbite’s — ‘bold rebuke and consequent suffering for the Truth.’ Gill notes that by Elijah is ‘designed’ John the Baptist.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the Elijah whom the scribes expected has already come, in John the Baptist, and has suffered as the Scriptures foretold. Christ thus settles the disciples’ question: the forerunner is not still to come but has come and gone, fulfilling Malachi’s promise in the spirit and power of the old prophet.

The shared, dominant note is the correspondence between the two careers. As Elijah boldly rebuked Ahab and Jezebel and suffered for it, so John rebuked Herod and Herodias and was put to death; what ‘was written of’ the one — the record of fearless witness and consequent suffering — found its fulfilment in the other. The witnesses see in this both the vindication of the scribes’ prophecy, rightly understood, and a quiet foreshadowing of Christ’s own path: if they did to the forerunner whatsoever they listed, the Son of man too must suffer many things at their hands. Gill notes that by Elijah is ‘designed’ John the Baptist; the witnesses agree the Elijah the scribes expected has already come and suffered, fulfilling Malachi in the spirit and power of the old prophet, and they see in the parallel careers — bold rebuke and consequent suffering — a quiet foreshadowing of Christ’s own path, that if they did to the forerunner as they listed, the Son of man too must suffer at their hands. Ellicott notes ‘as it is written of him’ is peculiar to Mark, pointing both to Malachi’s prophecy and to the parallel of bold rebuke and consequent suffering.
14:
Returning from the mount, Christ finds the nine disciples surrounded by a crowd and scribes disputing, unable to heal a possessed boy. The witnesses agree the scene contrasts the glory above with the failure and need below.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe it. Robertson notes the ‘scribes questioning’ is an item Mark alone gives, the professionals taking ‘keen interest in the failure of the nine’ and gleefully nagging them; Mark’s account (vv.14–29) is fuller than Matthew’s or Luke’s. Barnes notes the scribes attempted ‘to perplex and confound his followers’ in his absence.

The fathers draw out the contrast. Theophylact notes the Pharisees seized ‘the opportunity of the hour when Christ was not present’ to draw the disciples over; Bede observes the difference between the scribes, who showed no devotion, and the people, who were amazed and ran to salute him. Pseudo-Jerome: ‘envy is ever slaying the little ones.’

The Reformed Calvin and Puritan Henry note the timing: Christ ‘came very seasonably, when the disciples were embarrassed and run aground,’ the scribes having gained an apparent advantage. The shared, dominant note is the descent from glory to need: the Lord, just transfigured, returns to find his disciples baffled by a case they could not cure and harried by hostile scribes — a vivid picture, the witnesses agree, of the church’s perennial situation, where the powerlessness of his servants and the malice of his critics alike call for the present help of the Master. Pseudo-Jerome adds that ‘envy is ever slaying the little ones,’ and Henry that Christ came seasonably when the disciples were aground; the witnesses see in the descent from glory to need a vivid picture of the church’s perennial situation, where the powerlessness of his servants and the malice of his critics alike call for the present help of the Master, just come down from the mount. Theophylact notes the Pharisees seized ‘the opportunity of the hour when Christ was not present,’ and Bede the contrast between the devotionless scribes and the amazed people. Robertson notes the ‘scribes questioning’ is an item Mark alone gives, the professionals taking keen interest in the failure of the nine and gleefully nagging them.
15:
‘And straightway all the people, when they beheld him, were greatly amazed, and… saluted him.’ The few voices note the crowd’s amazement, peculiar to Mark, and speculate on its cause.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the detail. Robertson notes the ingressive aorist (‘were greatly amazed’): ‘the sudden and opportune appearance of Jesus… turned all eyes to him,’ and the people ‘began to welcome him.’ Ellicott asks whether ‘some lingering radiance, or some expression of divine joy… struck the disciples and the people’ as unlike his recent sadness (Mark 8:31). Clarke notes the cause ‘is not self-evident.’

Wesley likewise wonders whether ‘some unusual rays of majesty and glory… yet remained on his countenance,’ and Gill that he was personally known to many who had followed him from Galilee.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: at Christ’s sudden appearance the whole crowd was struck with amazement and ran to greet him. The grammatical commentators dwell on the suddenness and the opportune timing — he came just as the dispute raged and the disciples faltered — and several wonder, with Ellicott and Wesley, whether some afterglow of the Transfiguration still shone on his face, as once on Moses’s. The witnesses see in the people’s rush to salute him the contrast between the warm welcome of the multitude and the cold hostility of the scribes, and a token that, however his servants might fail, the presence of the Master drew all eyes and hopes to himself. Clarke notes the cause of the amazement ‘is not self-evident,’ and Gill that he was personally known to many from Galilee; the witnesses see in the people’s rush to salute him the contrast between the warm welcome of the multitude and the cold hostility of the scribes, and a token that, however his servants might fail, the presence of the Master drew all eyes and hopes to himself. Ellicott wonders whether some lingering radiance, or some expression of divine joy, struck the people as strangely unlike his recent sadness (Mark 8:31). Clarke notes the cause of the amazement ‘is not self-evident,’ while Robertson marks the ingressive aorist of the people’s sudden awe and rush to welcome him.
16:
‘And he asked them, What question ye with them?’ The three brief voices note Christ’s question that took hold of the situation.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that ‘Jesus had noticed the embarrassment of the nine and at once takes hold of the situation.’ Barnes glosses the question as asking the subject of their inquiry or debate. Gill suggests the question was put to the scribes, whom he saw closely pressing his disciples.

With only three brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Christ, arriving in the midst of the dispute, does not stand aloof but at once intervenes with a question that draws the conflict into the open: what were they debating with his disciples? Whether addressed to the scribes (Gill) or to the crowd, the question shows the Master taking command of a scene in which his servants had been embarrassed and harried.

The shared, dominant note is the readiness of Christ to engage the trouble his disciples could not handle. Where the nine had been confounded and the scribes had pressed their advantage, the Lord steps in and asks the cause, shifting the burden of the dispute from his faltering followers to himself. The witnesses see in this both his tender care for his embarrassed disciples — coming to their relief in their extremity — and his sovereign mastery of the situation, for the question that no one else could answer he is about to answer by the very deed the disciples had failed to perform. Barnes glosses the question as asking the subject of the debate, and Gill suggests it was put to the scribes pressing the disciples; the witnesses see in Christ’s ready intervention both his tender care for his embarrassed disciples, coming to their relief in their extremity, and his sovereign mastery of the scene, for the question none else could answer he is about to answer by the very deed the disciples had failed to perform. Robertson observes that Jesus ‘had noticed the embarrassment of the nine and at once takes hold of the situation,’ shifting the burden of the dispute to himself.
17:
‘Master, I have brought unto thee my son, which hath a dumb spirit.’ The witnesses agree the father’s account opens the case the disciples could not cure.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the affliction. Robertson notes the father ‘stepped out and gave the explanation… in direct and simple pathos’; Clarke explains the ‘dumb spirit’ deprived the boy of speech, the spirit itself being neither deaf nor dumb. Ellicott notes the ‘dumb spirit’ is peculiar to Mark. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 17:14).

The Reformed Calvin observes that Matthew describes the boy as ‘lunatic’ while Mark speaks of a dumb spirit; both agree he was possessed and at intervals furious, the disease (epilepsy) being affected by the moon yet the attack mixed up by Satan with natural means. Wesley notes the spirit ‘takes his speech from him.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: a distressed father, his appeal to the disciples having failed, lays his son’s case before Christ — a boy possessed by a spirit that robbed him of speech and threw him into grievous convulsions. The grammatical commentators dwell on the directness and pathos of the father’s words, and Calvin on the combination of natural disease and demonic malice. The witnesses see in the scene a desperate and chronic case, brought to Christ as the last resort, which will become the occasion both of a signal deliverance and of his teaching on the power of faith and prayer. Ellicott notes the ‘dumb spirit’ is peculiar to Mark, and Wesley that the spirit ‘takes his speech from him’; the witnesses see in the scene a desperate and chronic case brought to Christ as the last resort, after the disciples had failed, which will become the occasion both of a signal deliverance and of his teaching on the power of faith and prayer. Calvin observes that Matthew’s ‘lunatic’ and Mark’s ‘dumb spirit’ agree, the possession being mixed with a natural epilepsy affected by the moon. Robertson notes the father ‘stepped out and gave the explanation… in direct and simple pathos,’ laying his son’s case before Christ as the last resort.
18:
‘He teareth him… and he foameth, and gnasheth with his teeth, and pineth away: and I spake to thy disciples… and they could not.’ The few voices note the violence of the affliction and the disciples’ failure.

The grammatical-historical commentators paint the picture. Robertson notes each verb is ‘a graphic picture’ — the spirit ‘seizes him down’ (our word catalepsy), dashes him down, convulses, foams, grinds the teeth, withers him (compare James 1:11) — and that the disciples ‘did not have the strength to handle this case.’ Barnes lists the symptoms as marks of ‘violent derangement,’ the ‘gnashing’ and ‘pining’ peculiar to Mark (Ellicott). Clarke notes the boy was ‘sorely tortured.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the father details the dreadful symptoms — the convulsions, foaming, grinding of teeth, and wasting — and adds the painful confession that he had asked the disciples to cast it out, ‘and they could not.’ The grammatical commentators dwell on the vividness of Mark’s description, the touch of an eyewitness, and on the failure of the nine.

The shared, dominant note is the contrast between the violence of the demon and the impotence of the disciples. The case was severe and chronic, and the followers of Christ, who had cast out demons before (Mark 6:13), here met their match and failed. The witnesses see in this both the malignity of the powers of darkness, which yield to no half-hearted resistance, and the occasion for Christ to expose the cause of the disciples’ defeat — their want of faith and prayer — and to show that no case is too desperate for him who is about to deliver the boy. Ellicott notes the ‘gnashing’ and ‘pining’ are peculiar to Mark, and Clarke that the boy was ‘sorely tortured’; the witnesses see in the contrast between the demon’s violence and the disciples’ impotence the malignity of the powers of darkness, which yield to no half-hearted resistance, and the occasion for Christ to expose the cause of the disciples’ defeat — their want of faith and prayer.
19:
‘O faithless generation, how long shall I be with you?… bring him unto me.’ The few voices note Christ’s grief at unbelief and his readiness to act where others failed.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that ‘the disciples had failed and their unbelief had led to this fiasco’; even they were ‘part of the faithless generation,’ though ‘faithless’ here means unbelieving, not treacherous. ‘We can always come to Jesus when others fail us.’ Barnes refers to the faithless generation of Deuteronomy 32:20. Gill notes the rebuke included the father, who had taken part with the scribes against the disciples.

With only three brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Christ’s exclamation — ‘O faithless generation’ — is a lament over the unbelief that surrounded him: in the scribes, in the crowd, in the failing disciples, and in the wavering father. Yet the rebuke ends not in rejection but in mercy: ‘bring him unto me.’

The shared, dominant note is the mingling of grief and grace. The Lord grieves at the pervasive unbelief that had made his disciples powerless and the case seem hopeless, and his ‘how long shall I be with you?’ breathes the weariness of one bearing with a faithless generation. Yet in the same breath he commands the boy to be brought to him, taking up the case the disciples had bungled. The witnesses see in this both a solemn exposure of unbelief as the root of spiritual impotence and a comfort that, when his servants and all human helpers fail, Christ himself remains the sure resort of the needy. Barnes refers to the faithless generation of Deuteronomy 32:20, and Gill that the rebuke included the wavering father; the witnesses see in the verse a mingling of grief and grace — the Lord grieving at the unbelief that made his disciples powerless, yet in the same breath commanding the boy to be brought to him, a comfort that when his servants and all human helpers fail, Christ himself remains the sure resort of the needy. The rebuke ends not in rejection but in mercy, ‘bring him unto me,’ the Lord taking up the case the disciples had bungled.
20:
‘And when he saw him, straightway the spirit tare him; and he fell on the ground, and wallowed foaming.’ The witnesses note the demon’s violent rage at Christ’s presence.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the convulsion. Robertson notes the strong compound verb (‘convulsed’) used by Luke too, and that the ‘wallowing foaming’ is ‘a pitiful sight,’ the imperfect ‘was rolled.’ Ellicott notes the detail is ‘found only in St. Mark.’ Clarke says the demon, knowing ‘his time was short,’ had great rage.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson: ‘in proportion as the grace of Christ is seen to be nearer at hand… the fury of Satan is the more highly excited’; the presence of Christ ‘awakens him like the sound of a trumpet,’ and we should be prepared, lest our faith be disturbed when the approach of grace is met by greater violence from the enemy. Wesley notes the spirit ‘made his last grand effort to destroy him.’

The shared, dominant note is that the nearer the deliverance, the fiercer the demon’s rage. At the sight of Christ the spirit threw the boy into his worst convulsion yet, wallowing and foaming on the ground — not a sign of the demon’s strength but of his desperation, knowing his time was short. The witnesses, especially Calvin, draw the abiding lesson that Satan often rages most violently just when Christ is about to set the captive free, so that the believer must not be dismayed when the approach of grace stirs up the enemy’s last and worst assault. Clarke says the demon, knowing ‘his time was short,’ raged the more, and Wesley that the spirit ‘made his last grand effort to destroy him’; the witnesses, especially Calvin, draw the abiding lesson that Satan often rages most violently just when Christ is about to set the captive free, so that the believer must not be dismayed when the approach of grace stirs up the enemy’s last and worst assault. Ellicott notes the ‘wallowed foaming’ is a graphic touch found only in Mark, the demon doing the boy all possible harm in his rage at Christ’s nearness.
21:
‘How long is it ago since this came unto him? And he said, Of a child.’ The few voices note Christ’s question, drawing out the chronic, seemingly hopeless case.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes the question was asked ‘as if to bring into strong relief the chronic, and therefore seemingly hopeless, character of the possession,’ and that the circumstantial account of the falling ‘into the fire and into the waters’ is peculiar to Mark. Barnes points to the misery of fallen man (Psalms 51:5). Gill notes the question was asked ‘that the cure might be the more remarkable.’

The Reformed Calvin draws a careful inference: this affliction ‘was not inflicted on account of the sins of the individual, but was a secret judgment of God’; though even infants are not free from guilt, God’s chastisements ‘have sometimes hidden causes, and are intended to try our obedience’ (compare John 9:3).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ, like a physician taking the history of a case, asks how long the boy has been afflicted, and learns it has been from childhood. The question, the grammatical commentators note, served to set the deep-rooted and hopeless nature of the case in relief, so that the coming cure might be the more remarkable. The witnesses see in Christ’s patient inquiry both his true humanity and tender method, and, with Calvin, a reminder that such long affliction need not be the punishment of personal sin but may be a secret judgment of God, intended to try faith and to magnify, in the end, the grace that delivers. Barnes points to the misery of fallen man (Psalms 51:5), and Gill that the question was asked ‘that the cure might be the more remarkable’; the witnesses see in Christ’s patient inquiry both his true humanity and tender method, and, with Calvin, a reminder that such long affliction need not be the punishment of personal sin but may be a secret judgment of God, intended to try faith and to magnify the grace that delivers.
22:
‘If thou canst do any thing, have compassion on us, and help us.’ The witnesses agree the father’s plea betrays a faith weakened by the disciples’ failure.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the ‘if.’ Robertson notes the conditional ‘if thou canst’ suggests doubt whether the boy can be cured at all, the failure of the disciples having shaken the father’s faith; yet with ‘touching tenderness he makes the boy’s case his own’ — ‘help us’ — the ingressive aorist, ‘do it now.’ Ellicott hears the words ‘almost in the accents of despair.’ Clarke notes he had tried the disciples and found them powerless.

The Reformed Calvin presses the lesson: the father ‘renders little honour to Christ,’ supposing him ‘some prophet whose power was limited’; but ‘the first foundation of faith is to embrace the boundless power of God,’ and our faith must be ‘capable and prepared for receiving the desired favour.’ Wesley notes ‘have compassion on us — me as well as him.’

The shared, dominant note is that the father’s plea, though sincere and touching, was flawed by a hesitant ‘if thou canst’ that doubted Christ’s power. The grammatical commentators dwell on the pathos — a desperate father making his son’s misery his own — and Calvin on the theological fault, that to limit Christ’s power is to dishonour him. The witnesses see in the verse the setting for Christ’s reply, which will turn the father’s ‘if thou canst’ back upon him and make the cure hang not on Christ’s ability but on the father’s faith. Clarke notes the father had tried the disciples and found them powerless, and Wesley that ‘have compassion on us’ means ‘me as well as him’; the witnesses see in the plea both the pathos of a desperate father making his son’s misery his own and the theological fault Calvin marks, that to limit Christ’s power is to dishonour him, setting the stage for Christ’s reply that turns the ‘if’ back upon the father’s faith. Ellicott hears the words ‘almost in the accents of despair’ — could the Master prevail where the disciples had failed?
23:
‘If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth.’ The witnesses agree Christ throws the father’s ‘if’ back upon him, making the cure turn on faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the neat Greek. Robertson notes the article takes up the man’s very words — ‘as to the “if thou canst,” all things can to the one who believes’ — a ‘quick turn’ that challenges his faith. Barnes: ‘Jesus here demanded faith… God will do nothing in our favour without faith.’ Ellicott notes the best manuscripts omit ‘believe,’ reading the words as the sad surprise, ‘If thou canst…!’

The Reformed Calvin draws the ‘useful doctrine’: ‘it is not the Lord that prevents his benefits from flowing to us… but… the narrowness of our faith’ that makes them come ‘only in drops’; unbelief shuts up the heart. Clarke applies it: ‘Jesus is able; more, Jesus is willing; but we are not willing to give up our idols.’

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s reply transfers the ‘if’ from his own power to the father’s faith: the question is not whether Christ can, but whether the man can believe, for ‘all things are possible to him that believeth.’ The witnesses see in this both a rebuke of the doubt that would limit Christ and a great promise — that the boundless power of God is opened to faith. Calvin’s lesson stands for all: the measure of our receiving is the measure of our believing, and where his benefits come only in drops, the fault lies not in the Giver but in the narrowness of the faith that receives. Clarke applies it sharply: ‘Jesus is able; more, Jesus is willing; but we are not willing to give up our idols’; the witnesses see in Christ’s reply both a rebuke of the doubt that would limit him and a great promise — that the boundless power of God is opened to faith — Calvin’s lesson standing for all, that the measure of our receiving is the measure of our believing. Ellicott notes the best manuscripts omit ‘believe,’ reading the words as the sad surprise, ‘If thou canst…!’, a gentle rebuke of the doubt that would limit Christ.
24:
‘Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief.’ The witnesses cherish this cry as the perfect picture of a real but imperfect faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on it. Robertson calls it ‘an exact description of his mental and spiritual state’: ‘he still had faith, but craved more,’ the present imperative ‘help’ meaning continuous aid, from a word that means ‘to run at a cry for help.’ Barnes notes the man ‘felt the implied rebuke… and wept’; the best manuscripts (Ellicott, Clarke) omit ‘with tears.’ Clarke: ‘help me against it — give me power to believe.’

The Reformed Calvin expounds the paradox: the man ‘declares that he believes, and yet acknowledges himself to have unbelief’; since ‘our faith is never perfect,’ we are ‘partly unbelievers,’ yet God reckons us believers ‘on account of a small portion of faith,’ and we must pray him to correct the remains of infidelity. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem entry, on point, recalls Lazarus raised by the faith of his sisters supplying the dead man’s lack.

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced. The shared, dominant note is that the father’s cry holds together two truths every believer knows by experience: a genuine faith and a remaining unbelief. The witnesses see in ‘Lord, I believe; help my unbelief’ the model prayer of weak faith — honest enough to confess its weakness, believing enough to bring even that weakness to Christ for healing — and Calvin draws the comfort that God accepts and acts upon a faith that is small and mixed, provided it casts itself, with its very doubts, upon his boundless power. Wesley paraphrases the cry, ‘although my faith be so small that it might rather be termed unbelief, yet help me’; the witnesses see in ‘Lord, I believe; help my unbelief’ the model prayer of weak faith — honest enough to confess its weakness, believing enough to bring even that weakness to Christ — and Calvin draws the comfort that God accepts and acts upon a faith that is small and mixed, provided it casts itself upon his boundless power. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem entry, on point, recalls Lazarus raised by the faith of his sisters supplying the dead man’s own lack of faith.
25:
‘Thou dumb and deaf spirit, I charge thee, come out of him, and enter no more into him.’ The witnesses note Christ’s authoritative word and its tender adaptation to the father’s weak faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the command. Clarke lays ‘emphasis on the pronoun’: the spirit had resisted the disciples, ‘now I command thee,’ and argues from the address that this was a real demon, not mere disease — ‘is the doctrine of demoniacal influence false? then Christ would never have countenanced it.’ Robertson notes Jesus addresses the demon ‘as a separate being… the cause… of the boy’s misfortune.’ Ellicott notes ‘enter no more into him’ is a tender adaptation to the father, who had seen so many relapses.

Gill notes Christ acted ‘when the people came running together,’ not to court a crowd but because the moment was come. Wesley: ‘when Jesus spake, the devil heard, though the child could not.’

The shared, dominant note is the divine authority of Christ’s word, where the disciples had failed. He speaks not in prayer but in command, charging the spirit to come out and never return — a word, the grammatical commentators argue, that treats the demon as a real and personal cause, not a mere figure for disease. The witnesses see in ‘enter no more into him’ a gracious condescension to the father’s fear of relapse, assuring him the cure would be complete and permanent; and in the whole command the sovereign power of the Lord of the spirits, before whom even the most obstinate demon must yield. Gill notes Christ acted ‘when the people came running together,’ not to court a crowd but because the moment was come, and Wesley that ‘when Jesus spake, the devil heard, though the child could not’; the witnesses see in ‘enter no more into him’ a gracious condescension to the father’s fear of relapse, and in the whole command the sovereign power of the Lord of the spirits, before whom even the most obstinate demon must yield. Clarke argues from the address — ‘thou deaf and dumb spirit’ — that this was a real demon, not mere disease, which Christ would never have countenanced as a falsehood.
26:
‘And the spirit cried, and rent him sore, and came out of him: and he was as one dead.’ The few voices note the demon’s violent departure and the boy’s death-like state.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe it. Robertson notes the boy ‘became as one dead… from the violence of the spasm,’ the demon doing ‘him all possible harm in leaving him.’ Ellicott notes the ‘rent him sore’ (a spasm convulsing the frame), the corpse-like falling, and the cry of ‘the many’ that he was dead, are all peculiar to Mark. Gill notes the demon, ‘filled with wrath,’ shook and tore him before leaving.

Wesley observes ‘so does even the body sometimes suffer, when God comes to deliver the soul from Satan.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the demon, forced out, vented his last malice in a violent convulsion that left the boy lying like a corpse, so that many said, ‘He is dead.’ The grammatical commentators dwell on Mark’s vivid, eyewitness detail — the cry, the rending, the death-like stillness — and on the spite of the departing spirit, who did the boy all the harm he could in going.

The shared, dominant note is that the deliverance came through what looked like death. The demon’s parting fury was so great that the onlookers thought the boy slain; yet this apparent death was the threshold of life, for, as the next verse shows, Christ would take him by the hand and raise him up. The witnesses see in the scene a picture of how the powers of darkness rage most violently at the moment of their defeat, and how the soul Christ delivers may pass, as it were, through death to newness of life. Gill notes the demon, ‘filled with wrath,’ shook and tore the boy before leaving, and Wesley that the body sometimes suffers when God delivers the soul; the witnesses see in the scene a picture of how the powers of darkness rage most violently at the moment of their defeat, and how the soul Christ delivers may pass, as it were, through death to newness of life, for the apparent death was the threshold of life.
27:
‘But Jesus took him by the hand, and lifted him up; and he arose.’ Only two brief voices comment, so the synthesis is short; they note the gentle, raising touch of Christ.

The Reformed Gill notes simply that Christ ‘took hold of the hand of the child,’ and the grammatical-historical Barnes points to the upholding hand of God (Isaiah 41:13).

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is clear and agreed. The boy, lying as one dead after the demon’s violent departure, is raised by the touch of Christ: he takes him by the hand, lifts him up, and the child stands. What the demon had left like a corpse, the Lord restores to life and strength by a gesture of tender power.

The shared, dominant note is the gentleness and the might of Christ’s raising hand. The same Lord whose word had cast out the spirit now stoops to take the prostrate boy by the hand and lift him up — a touch the older writers love to set beside other scenes where Christ raises the fallen and the dead. The witnesses see in it both the completeness of the cure, the boy not merely freed but restored, and a figure of the grace that lays hold of those whom sin and Satan have cast down as good as dead, and by the hand of Christ raises them up to newness of life; for, as Barnes notes, it is the upholding hand of God that says, ‘Fear not; I will help thee.’ Gill notes Christ ‘took hold of the hand of the child,’ and the witnesses see in the raising touch both the completeness of the cure — the boy not merely freed but restored — and a figure of the grace that lays hold of those whom sin and Satan have cast down as good as dead, and by the hand of Christ raises them up to newness of life, the upholding hand that says ‘Fear not; I will help thee.’ Barnes points to the upholding hand of God (Isaiah 41:13), the same Lord whose word cast out the spirit now stooping to lift the prostrate boy.
28:
‘Why could not we cast him out?’ The two voices note the disciples’ private question after their failure.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that ‘indoors the nine disciples seek an explanation for their colossal failure’; they had cast out demons before, and were puzzled. He notes the Greek interrogative (‘why,’ as in Mark 2:16). Gill notes they came into the house, perhaps the father’s, for ‘retirement and refreshment.’

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Withdrawn from the crowd into a house, the disciples ask their Master privately why they had failed where they had succeeded before. The question is honest and troubled: they had been given power over unclean spirits (Mark 6:7) and had used it, yet in this case they were powerless.

The shared, dominant note is the disciples’ perplexity at their own impotence. Their private question shows both their humility — they bring their failure to Christ rather than excuse it — and their ignorance of its cause, which the next verse will supply. The witnesses see in the scene a lesson for all Christ’s servants: past success is no guarantee of present power, and when we fail in the work we have done before, the right course is not to despair or to blame the difficulty of the case, but, like the nine, to come apart to Christ and ask him to show us the secret of our weakness — which he will trace, not to any lack in himself, but to the prayerlessness and little faith of his servants. Robertson notes the Greek interrogative ‘why’ (as in Mark 2:16), and Gill that they came into the house for retirement; the witnesses see in the disciples’ private question both their humility, bringing their failure to Christ rather than excusing it, and a lesson that past success is no guarantee of present power, the right course being to come apart to Christ and ask the secret of our weakness. They had been given power over unclean spirits (Mark 6:7) and had used it, yet in this case were powerless, and bring their perplexity to Christ.
29:
‘This kind can come forth by nothing, but by prayer.’ The witnesses agree the disciples’ failure was want of prayer and faith, and debate the textual addition ‘and fasting.’

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the cause. Robertson notes the addition ‘and fasting’ is wanting in the two best manuscripts and is ‘a late addition’; ‘prayer is what the nine had failed to use… they were powerless because they were prayerless,’ their ‘self-complacency spelled defeat,’ and Matthew adds ‘because of your little faith’ (Matthew 17:20). Ellicott likewise notes the best manuscripts omit the words, perhaps added ‘from an ascetic tendency’ (compare 1 Corinthians 7:5).

Clarke reads the demon as ‘an emblem of deeply rooted vices… over which the conquest is not generally obtained, but through extraordinary humiliations,’ and Gill notes Matthew assigns the further reason of their unbelief.

The shared, dominant note is that some powers of darkness yield only to prayer — and, as Matthew adds, to faith. The grammatical commentators judge ‘and fasting’ a later insertion, and locate the disciples’ failure in their self-confidence and prayerlessness, trusting (as Swete says) to ‘a semi-magical power’ rather than to living dependence on God. The witnesses see in the saying both a humbling of the disciples — their defeat was their own fault, not the case’s hopelessness — and an abiding lesson, that the deepest strongholds of evil, whether demons or deep-rooted sins, are overcome not by routine or technique but by earnest, believing prayer that keeps the soul in dependence on the power of God. Robertson judges ‘and fasting’ a late addition wanting in the best manuscripts, locating the disciples’ failure in their self-confidence and prayerlessness.
30:
‘They departed thence, and passed through Galilee… for he taught his disciples’ of his coming death. The witnesses agree Christ sought privacy to prepare the Twelve, joining sorrow with the hope of resurrection.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the secrecy. Robertson reads ‘he would not that any man should know it’ — Jesus ‘avoiding public work’ now, ‘no longer the hero of Galilee’ (compare Mark 7:24). Wesley notes he ‘wanted to be alone with them… to instruct them fully concerning his sufferings.’ Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 17:22).

The fathers mark the purpose. Theophylact holds Christ inserted the discourse on his Passion ‘after miracles, lest it should be thought that he suffered because he could not help it,’ and adds the joy of the resurrection ‘that we may learn that joys come on after struggles.’ Bede notes he ‘always mingles together sorrowful and joyful things,’ that the disciples might bear the sorrow with prepared minds.

The Puritan Henry observes Christ passed through Galilee privately because ‘the time of his sufferings drew nigh,’ and his heart was set on his suffering-work. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, withdrawing from the crowds, devoted himself to preparing the Twelve for his death — foretelling it plainly yet always joining the promise of his rising. The witnesses see in this both the deliberateness of his Passion, which he embraced wittingly and not from weakness, and his tender care to brace his disciples beforehand, that the coming sorrow might not overwhelm them but be borne with minds forewarned and steadied by the hope of resurrection. Pseudo-Jerome notes that Capernaum means ‘the city of consolation,’ agreeing with the promise of the resurrection; the witnesses see in Christ’s withdrawal both the deliberateness of his Passion, embraced wittingly and not from weakness, and his tender care to brace his disciples beforehand, that the coming sorrow might be borne with minds forewarned and steadied by the hope of his rising on the third day. Bede notes that Christ ‘always mingles together sorrowful and joyful things,’ that the disciples might bear the coming sorrow with prepared minds.
31:
‘The Son of man is delivered into the hands of men, and they shall kill him; and after that he is killed, he shall rise the third day.’ The few voices note the renewed, plain prediction.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the teaching. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘he taught’ gives the reason for the secrecy: he was ‘renewing again definitely the prediction of his death… some six months ahead’ (Mark 8:31), foretelling his resurrection ‘after three days.’ Barnes glosses ‘is delivered’ as given ‘to make atonement by his sufferings and death.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is on point, urging that Christ ‘came to his passion willingly… lest the whole world should perish in its sins.’ Gill notes Christ taught what he had ‘some little time before suggested.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ repeats, plainly and privately, the prediction of his betrayal, death, and resurrection. The grammatical commentators note the renewal of the warning given before (Mark 8:31), now pressed home as the time drew near, and the constant joining of the rising on the third day. The witnesses see in the prediction both the deliberateness of his Passion — he is ‘delivered’ not by mischance but by the determinate counsel of God, to make atonement — and, with Cyril, his willingness, going to death not because he could not escape but because the world’s salvation required it; and in the promised resurrection the steady hope set, even here, against the coming sorrow. Robertson notes he was renewing the prediction of his death given before (Mark 8:31), now pressed home as the time drew near, with the rising on the third day.
32:
‘But they understood not that saying, and were afraid to ask him.’ The witnesses agree the disciples’ continued incomprehension was mingled with fear.

The grammatical-historical commentators analyse it. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘continued not to understand’: they were ‘agnostics on the subject of the death and resurrection even after the Transfiguration,’ and ‘afraid to ask him’ perhaps ‘with a bitter memory’ of the rebuke ‘Satan’ (Mark 8:33). Ellicott notes the analysis of their thoughts is ‘not in St. Matthew but… in St. Luke.’ Barnes refers to their later fuller understanding.

The Wesleyan Wesley explains they ‘did not understand how to reconcile the death of our Saviour… with their notions of his temporal kingdom.’ Gill notes they grasped the plain words about his death, which made them ‘exceeding sorry,’ but not how it could consist with his being the Messiah.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the disciples did not understand the prediction and were afraid to ask. The grammatical commentators dwell on the persistence of their dullness even after the mount, and on the fear that held them back — perhaps the memory of Peter’s rebuke when he last protested the cross. The witnesses see in their incomprehension the grip of the popular hope of a temporal Messiah, which could not accommodate a dying and rising Christ, and in their fear a reluctance to probe a truth that grieved them. The whole, Ellicott notes, is one of Mark’s and Luke’s honest analyses of the disciples’ minds — a comfort to all who are slow to grasp the deep things of God, with whom the Lord nonetheless patiently bore. Clarke notes the three who had seen the Transfiguration may have understood more than the nine, and Gill that they grasped the plain words about his death, which made them ‘exceeding sorry’; the witnesses see in their incomprehension the grip of the popular hope of a temporal Messiah, which could not accommodate a dying and rising Christ, and in their fear a reluctance to probe a truth that grieved them.
33:
‘What was it that ye disputed among yourselves by the way?’ The witnesses agree Christ exposes the disciples’ dispute about greatness.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Robertson notes they had been disputing ‘not about the coming death of the Master, but about the relative rank of each’ in the kingdom they expected; Jesus ‘suspected the truth’ and pushes the matter ‘deeper’ (compare Matthew 18:1). Clarke and Barnes refer to the parallels (Luke 9:46). Gill notes they were in the house, perhaps Peter’s.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ, in the house, asks the disciples what they had been arguing about on the road. The grammatical commentators note the painful contrast — while their Master spoke of his death, they were disputing which of them should be greatest in the kingdom they still imagined to be a temporal one.

The shared, dominant note is the Lord’s gentle but searching exposure of the disciples’ ambition. He had heard, or discerned, their wrangling, and now draws it into the open with a question, that he might correct the spirit beneath it. The witnesses see in the scene both the persistence of carnal ambition even in the chosen Twelve, and so soon after the prediction of the cross, and the patient method of Christ, who does not at once denounce but asks, that the disciples might themselves feel the unworthiness of their dispute — preparing the way for the great lesson that the path to true greatness in his kingdom is the path of lowly service, which the following verses unfold. Clarke and Barnes refer to the parallels (Luke 9:46), and Gill that they were in the house, perhaps Peter’s; the witnesses see in the scene both the persistence of carnal ambition even in the chosen Twelve, and so soon after the prediction of the cross, and the patient method of Christ, who does not at once denounce but asks, that the disciples might themselves feel the unworthiness of their dispute. Robertson notes the painful contrast — while their Master spoke of his death, they disputed which of them should be greatest in the kingdom they still imagined temporal.
34:
‘But they held their peace: for by the way they had disputed… who should be the greatest.’ The few voices note the disciples’ shamed silence.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that ‘put thus to them, they felt ashamed that the Master had discovered their jealous rivalry’; it was ‘not a mere abstract query… but a canker in their hearts.’ Ellicott renders it more simply ‘who was the greatest.’ Wesley glosses the dispute as over who should be ‘prime minister in his kingdom.’ Gill notes they held their peace, ‘fearing a reprimand for their vanity.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the disciples, asked what they had argued about, fell silent in shame, for they had been disputing which of them should be greatest. Their silence, the grammatical commentators note, was itself a confession: they were ashamed that their Master had uncovered the jealous rivalry festering among them.

The shared, dominant note is that the disciples’ silence betrayed the guilt of their ambition. The dispute over rank was no harmless speculation but, as Robertson says, ‘a canker in their hearts’ — a craving for the chief place in the kingdom they still misconceived. The witnesses see in their shamed quiet both the working of conscience, which would not let them defend so unworthy a wrangle before the face of the Lord who had just spoken of his cross, and the occasion for Christ’s tender correction; for he does not now upbraid them, but sits down and teaches them the great reversal of his kingdom, that the way to be first is to be last of all and servant of all. Ellicott renders it more simply ‘who was the greatest,’ and Gill that they held their peace ‘fearing a reprimand for their vanity’; the witnesses see in their shamed quiet both the working of conscience, which would not let them defend so unworthy a wrangle before the Lord who had just spoken of his cross, and the occasion for Christ’s tender correction in the great reversal of his kingdom. Wesley glosses the dispute as over who should be ‘prime minister in his kingdom,’ a canker of ambition Christ would correct with the lesson of lowly service.
35:
‘If any man desire to be first, the same shall be last of all, and servant of all.’ The few voices note Christ’s deliberate teaching of the great reversal.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the deliberate action: ‘he sat down and called the twelve,’ giving them ‘the rule of greatness’ — the would-be first must be ‘last of all and servant of all’ — a saying he repeated at other times (Mark 10:43). Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 20:26). Gill notes he sat down ‘as their master, as one having authority.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ, sitting down as a teacher with authority, lays down the law of true greatness in his kingdom — the reverse of the world’s. The man who would be first must take the lowest place and become the servant of all. The grammatical commentators note the deliberateness of the lesson and its repetition elsewhere, marking it as a settled principle of his kingdom.

The shared, dominant note is the inversion of the disciples’ ambition. They had disputed who should be greatest; Christ answers not by forbidding the desire for greatness but by redefining it: greatness is found in service, primacy in lowliness. The witnesses see in this the abiding charter of Christian honour — that the way up is down, and the truly first in his kingdom are those who make themselves last and serve their brethren — a lesson the Master would seal in the next verse with a little child, and at last embody in his own death as the servant of all who gave his life a ransom for many. Barnes refers to the parallel (Matthew 20:26), and Gill that Christ sat down ‘as their master, as one having authority’; the witnesses see in this the abiding charter of Christian honour — that the way up is down, and the truly first in his kingdom are those who make themselves last and serve their brethren — a lesson the Master would seal with a child and at last embody in his own death as the servant of all.
36:
‘And he took a child, and set him in the midst of them: and when he had taken him in his arms.’ The few voices note the child set as an object lesson of humility.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the participle (‘taken him in his arms’) occurs only here and in Mark 10:16, meaning either taking up or folding the arms round the child — ‘the latter somewhat the more probable.’ Gill notes the child was ‘in the house… called and set by him.’ The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With only a few brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. To teach the disciples, who had been disputing about greatness, Christ takes a little child, sets him in their midst, and folds him in his arms. The act is itself the lesson: the child, lowly and unambitious, is the pattern of the greatness Christ commends, and the embrace shows the Lord’s tender regard for the lowly.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ teaches humility by a living emblem. Where the disciples sought the highest place, he sets before them the lowest — a child, who makes no claim to rank — and by taking the child in his arms shows that such lowliness is dear to him. The witnesses see in the gesture both a rebuke of the disciples’ ambition and a revelation of Christ’s heart toward the humble; for, as the next verse will declare, to receive one such child in his name is to receive him, and through him the Father who sent him. The embrace of the child is thus the silent sermon that prepares for the saying that follows. Gill notes the child was ‘in the house… called and set by him’; the witnesses see in the gesture both a rebuke of the disciples’ ambition and a revelation of Christ’s heart toward the humble, for, as the next verse will declare, to receive one such child in his name is to receive him, and through him the Father who sent him — the embrace of the child being the silent sermon that prepares for the saying that follows.
37:
‘Whosoever shall receive one of such children in my name, receiveth me… and him that sent me.’ The few voices note the ascending chain from the child to Christ to the Father.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Gill explains the ‘child’ means ‘any believer who is like to such a child for humility,’ the meanest believer comparable to a little child. Robertson notes it was ‘an object lesson to the arrogant conceit of the twelve… contending for primacy.’ Ellicott draws out the climax: to love a child for Christ’s sake is to share his spirit, and to receive him is to enter ‘fellowship with him who is the Supreme and Eternal Love’ (compare John 14:23).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ binds together, in an ascending chain, the lowly child, himself, and the Father. To receive one such child — or, as Gill notes, the humblest believer who is like a child — in his name is to receive Christ, and to receive Christ is to receive the Father who sent him.

The shared, dominant note is the dignity Christ confers on the lowly and on lowly service. The disciples had despised the small in their reach for the great; Christ teaches that the smallest act of love to the meanest of his, done in his name, reaches up through him to God himself. The witnesses see in this both a rebuke of the disciples’ ambition and a transforming of the whole scale of greatness: the way to fellowship with the Supreme Love is not to grasp at primacy but to receive, for Christ’s sake, the child and the lowly believer — for in them, and in such service, Christ and the Father are received.
