38:
‘Master, we saw one casting out devils in thy name, and he followeth not us: and we forbad him.’ The witnesses agree John’s report betrays a narrow, exclusive zeal that Christ will correct.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the man. Barnes and Clarke suggest he was ‘one of John’s disciples, or one of the seventy’ (Luke 10:1), not a mere exorcist using the name as a pretence. Robertson notes John told this ‘expecting praise’ but perhaps with a doubt raised by Christ’s words about receiving in his name; one must know ‘the difference between loyalty to Jesus and stickling over one’s own narrow prejudices.’ Lightfoot conjectures the man cast out demons ‘in the name of the Messiah’ out of ignorance, not contempt.

The fathers probe the motive. Bede holds ‘no one is to be driven away from that partial goodness which he possesses already, but rather… stirred up to that which he has not.’ Pseudo-Chrysostom and Theophylact note it was ‘not from jealousy or envy’ that John forbade him, but a wish that all should follow Christ; yet the Lord extends his name even by the unworthy. Augustine harmonises with ‘he that is not with me is against me’ (Luke 11:23).

The Reformed Calvin and Wesleyan Wesley draw the warning against bigotry. The shared, dominant note is that John, fresh from the lesson on receiving the lowly, reveals the opposite spirit — forbidding a man who did Christ’s work because he did not follow the apostolic company. The witnesses see in this the perennial fault of sectarian narrowness, which Christ is about to rebuke with the great saying that he who is not against us is on our part. Lightfoot conjectures the man cast out demons ‘in the name of the Messiah’ out of ignorance, not contempt, and Calvin that he had rashly seized an office not given him; the witnesses see in John’s report, fresh from the lesson on receiving the lowly, the opposite spirit of sectarian narrowness, which Christ is about to rebuke with the great saying that he who is not against us is on our part.
39:
‘Forbid him not: for there is no man which shall do a miracle in my name, that can lightly speak evil of me.’ The witnesses agree Christ forbids the disciples’ narrowness and welcomes any genuine work done in his name.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes the present imperative — ‘stop hindering him’ as John had been doing. Barnes explains the man’s power to work a miracle ‘by that he gave evidence that he could not soon be found among the enemies of Jesus’; the word ‘lightly’ means quickly. Ellicott cites Moses’s answer to Joshua about Eldad and Medad — ‘would God that all the Lord’s people were prophets’ (Numbers 11:29) — and Paul’s joy that Christ was every way preached (Philippians 1:18).

The Reformed Calvin notes Christ did not approve the man’s irregular course but bids the disciples bear with it and rejoice, since ‘when by any occurrence God is glorified, we ought to… rejoice.’ Clarke warns that ‘a spirit of bigotry has little countenance from these passages.’

The shared, dominant note is Christ’s rebuke of exclusive zeal and his welcome of genuine service wherever found. He does not say the man was wholly right, but forbids the disciples to hinder one who, working a true miracle in his name, gave proof that he was no enemy. The witnesses see in this a charter against bigotry: the true disciple is to hinder no one really doing Christ’s work, for the very doing of it in his name binds the doer to him; and, as Calvin and Clarke insist, where God is glorified by any means, we are to rejoice rather than to forbid, mindful that loyalty to Christ is a larger thing than conformity to our own party. 40:
‘For he that is not against us is on our part.’ The witnesses agree this saying complements, rather than contradicts, the word that he who is not with Christ is against him.

The grammatical-historical commentators reconcile the two. Robertson notes the complement in Matthew 12:30 (‘he that is not with me is against me’): ‘both are needed,’ for some ‘imagine that they are really for Christ who refuse to take a stand… with him.’ Clarke and Gill read it as a proverb: one not openly against you, doing the same work, may fairly be reckoned on your side. Wesley distinguishes the two sayings: against Satan there is ‘no neutrality,’ but in judging men’s characters we are ‘charitably to hope’ the best of those who do not oppose Christ’s cause.

The Reformed Calvin guards the balance: Christ does not license ‘rash men’ to disturb the church’s order, but affirms that those who ‘do no harm… are useful and profitable,’ and that we ‘ought not to raise a quarrel till we are constrained.’

The shared, dominant note is the generous principle of judging others: one who is not against Christ and his people, but advances the same cause, is to be counted a friend. The witnesses set this beside the stern word of Matthew 12:30 and find no contradiction: toward Satan and in our own hearts there is no neutral ground, but toward our fellow-labourers we are to be charitable, hoping the best of those who do Christ’s work in their own way. As Wesley sums it, ‘we are to be rigorous in judging ourselves, and candid in judging each other’ — a rule that at once forbids bigotry and self-deceiving neutrality. Clarke and Gill read it as a proverb, that one not openly against you may fairly be reckoned a friend, and Wesley that toward Satan there is ‘no neutrality’; the witnesses set this beside the stern word of Matthew 12:30 and find no contradiction — no neutral ground toward Satan or in our own hearts, but charity toward our fellow-labourers, rigorous in judging ourselves and candid in judging each other.
41:
‘Whosoever shall give you a cup of water… because ye belong to Christ… shall not lose his reward.’ The witnesses agree the least kindness done to Christ’s for his sake is surely rewarded.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the ease and the ground. Barnes notes ‘how easy it is to be a Christian’ — to give a cup of water — but it must be ‘in his name… from love to the Saviour,’ not mere kindness; ‘the humblest acts… will be graciously noticed… and rewarded.’ Robertson notes ‘because ye are Christ’s’ (predicate genitive) is ‘the bond of universal brotherhood… over the lines of nation, race, class, sex’ (compare Romans 8:9). Ellicott notes the reiteration of Matthew 10:42 in a new context, Christ’s manner of repeating blessings.

The Puritan Henry marks the honour of belonging to Christ, and that ‘the relieving of Christ’s poor… will turn a good account.’ Wesley notes the discourse resumes from v.37.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on eternal life, tangential. The shared, dominant note is that the smallest service rendered to a believer for Christ’s sake is accepted and rewarded by Christ. The witnesses dwell on the lowliness of the deed — a cup of cold water — and on the all-important motive: it must be done because the recipient belongs to Christ. So humble is the service required, and so sure the reward, that none of Christ’s people is too poor to do good, and no act of love to them, however small, is forgotten by him. Robertson notes ‘because ye are Christ’s’ is the bond of universal brotherhood, over the lines of nation, race, class, and sex (Romans 8:9).
42:
‘Whosoever shall offend one of these little ones that believe in me, it is better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck.’ The few voices note the dreadful warning against causing the weak to stumble.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the ‘little ones.’ Gill explains they are not children in age but ‘the meanest person that believes in Christ,’ mean in their own and others’ eyes; Christ speaks of his disciples and followers. Barnes refers to the parallel and the millstone (Matthew 18:6). Wesley notes the contrast: against offending ‘the very least Christian.’

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here, on the parable of the sower, is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: to cause one of the humblest believers to stumble is a sin so grievous that a violent death — drowning with a millstone about the neck — would be better for the offender than to face the judgment such offence incurs. The grammatical commentators stress that the ‘little ones’ are not infants but the lowly disciples, whom the world and even fellow-believers may despise.

The shared, dominant note is the immense value Christ sets on the meanest of his, and the terror of the warning against harming them. Having taught the disciples to receive the lowly and to forbid no genuine servant, he now adds the dark obverse: to offend — to trip into sin or drive from the faith — one of these little ones who believe in him is a crime better punished by drowning than left for the judgment of God. The witnesses see in this both the tender care of Christ for the weakest of his flock and a solemn caution to all who, by word or example, might become a stumbling-block to them. 43:
‘If thy hand offend thee, cut it off: it is better for thee to enter into life maimed, than… to go into hell.’ The witnesses agree on the call to ruthless self-denial, and the fathers add the figure of cutting off harmful friends.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the imagery and the warnings. Robertson and Ellicott render ‘hell’ as Gehenna (the Valley of Hinnom, where refuse burned), distinct from Hades, ‘the unquenchable fire’ (compare Matthew 18:8). Clarke notes the textual doubt over the repeated ‘fire that never shall be quenched’ clause. Lightfoot adds a rabbinic parallel on cutting off the offending hand.

The fathers read the hand morally. Bede and Chrysostom take the ‘hand’ of an ‘intimate friend… necessary to us,’ whom, if he would hurt our soul, we must ‘drive away from our society,’ for ‘nothing is so hurtful as mischievous society’; the Gloss: better to enter life without a friend than with him into hell. Pseudo-Jerome and Augustine meditate on the worm and the fire as the soul’s remorse.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads it of whatever is as dear or useful as a hand — person or thing — that hinders us in the ways of God: ‘renounce all intercourse with him.’ The shared, dominant note is the urgency of radical self-denial: whatever, however dear, leads us into sin must be cut off, for it is better to enter life deprived of it than to keep it and be cast into the unquenchable fire. The witnesses divide only on the reference — literal members and besetting sins, or harmful friends — but agree that no earthly good, however precious, is worth the loss of the soul. Robertson and Ellicott render ‘hell’ as Gehenna, the Valley of Hinnom where refuse burned, distinct from Hades, ‘the unquenchable fire’ (Matthew 18:8).
44:
‘Where their worm dieth not, and the fire is not quenched.’ The few voices expound the dread image from Isaiah, while the grammatical commentators note its textual history.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the source. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson root it in Isaiah 66:24, the closing vision of the prophet, where the carcases of transgressors are devoured by an undying worm and unquenched fire — imagery ‘drawn from the slaughter of earthly battles’ and thence ‘perpetuated throughout eternity.’ Robertson notes the oldest manuscripts omit verses 44 and 46 as ‘a mere repetition of verse 48,’ so the numbering is retained though the words are not original here.

Gill expounds the figure: ‘by their worm is meant their conscience,’ gnawing continually, and the fire the wrath of God; both perpetual. Wesley reads the worm as the soul’s own ‘pride, self-will, desire, malice, envy, shame, sorrow, despair’ that ‘dieth not,’ and the fire, ‘material or infinitely worse,’ not quenched for ever.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the doom of those cast into Gehenna is described by Isaiah’s twin figures of the undying worm and the unquenchable fire. The grammatical commentators judge the clause a genuine repetition borrowed here from verse 48, but agree on its meaning; and the older writers interpret the worm as the gnawing conscience and the fire as the wrath of God — both, by the prophet’s own words, perpetual. The witnesses see in the saying Christ’s solemn sanction of the reality of eternal punishment, the strongest possible motive for the radical self-denial he has just enjoined. Gill expounds the worm as the gnawing conscience and the fire as the wrath of God, both perpetual, and Wesley reads the worm as the soul’s own pride, malice, and despair; the witnesses, though they judge the clause a repetition carried here from verse 48, agree on its meaning and see in it Christ’s solemn sanction of the reality of eternal punishment, the strongest motive for the self-denial he has enjoined. Barnes roots the figure in Isaiah 66:24, the carcases of transgressors devoured by an undying worm and unquenched fire, perpetuated throughout eternity.
45:
‘And if thy foot offend thee, cut it off.’ Only one voice comments, so the synthesis is brief; Gill reads the ‘foot’ as another figure for a harmful connection.

The Reformed Gill explains the ‘foot’ of those who are to a man ‘as the foot is to the body, the support of them… through whom they have their maintenance and subsistence’; yet if such become a means of causing him to stumble, or of leading him out of the ways of Christ, ‘their company is to be shunned.’

With only one brief voice the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance follows the pattern of the foregoing verse. As the hand stood for a dear and useful friend, so the foot stands for another such support — a connection on which a man leans for his livelihood and comfort — which must nonetheless be cut off if it leads him into sin or away from Christ.

The shared, dominant note, consistent with the surrounding verses, is the principle of ruthless self-denial applied to a second figure. Whatever upholds us in this life, however much we depend on it, is to be renounced rather than suffered to drag us into perdition. The witness sees in the ‘foot,’ as in the ‘hand’ and the ‘eye,’ a comprehensive warning: no relationship, occupation, or support is so necessary that it may be kept at the price of the soul; better to enter life maimed of every earthly prop than, retaining them all, to be cast into the unquenchable fire. The repetition drives home that the call to cut off the offending member admits no exception, however dear or useful the member may be. 46:
‘Where their worm dieth not, and the fire is not quenched.’ The two voices note the repeated refrain and its bearing on the eternity of punishment.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes simply ‘see on 44’ — the clause, like verse 44, is omitted by the oldest manuscripts as a repetition of verse 48, so the numbering is kept though the words are not original here. The Reformed Gill notes the Persic version renders it ‘because from thence there can be no deliverance,’ which he judges a good interpretation, since the figures ‘design the eternity of hell torments.’

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The refrain of the undying worm and unquenchable fire is repeated, as a solemn drumbeat, after the warning about the foot, as after the hand. The grammatical commentators judge the clause a genuine repetition carried over from verse 48; the Reformed reading takes it as designedly emphatic, underscoring that the torments of hell admit of no end or deliverance.

The shared, dominant note is the deliberate reiteration of the doom of Gehenna to drive home the urgency of self-denial. By repeating the dreadful words of Isaiah after each member to be cut off, Christ presses upon the conscience the reality and the perpetuity of the punishment that awaits the unrepentant. The witnesses see in the refrain — whatever its textual history — a sanction of the seriousness with which the offending hand, foot, and eye must be dealt with: the fire is not quenched, the worm does not die, and from that place, as Gill notes, there is no deliverance; therefore no sacrifice in this life is too great to escape it. The witnesses, though they judge the clause a repetition carried from verse 48, agree it underscores that the torments of hell admit of no end or deliverance; by repeating the dreadful words after each member to be cut off, Christ presses upon the conscience the reality and perpetuity of the punishment awaiting the unrepentant, so that no sacrifice in this life is too great to escape it.
47:
‘If thine eye offend thee, pluck it out: it is better for thee to enter into the kingdom of God with one eye, than… to be cast into hell fire.’ The few voices note the climactic figure and the dearest member.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the climax. Robertson notes Mark has ‘kingdom of God’ where Matthew 18:9 has ‘life,’ and the vernacular word for ‘one-eyed.’ Gill explains the ‘eye’ signifies what is ‘dearest to man… most beloved… the most intimate acquaintance and bosom friends,’ which must yet be parted with when they prove a snare. Barnes refers to the parallel.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem here treats baptism by water and the Spirit, and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the eye, the dearest and tenderest member, stands for whatever is most beloved — the closest friend or fondest object — which must be plucked out if it leads to sin. The climax of the three figures (hand, foot, eye) presses the lesson to its sharpest point: even what we hold most precious is to be surrendered rather than suffered to bar us from the kingdom of God.

The shared, dominant note is the absolute priority of the kingdom over every earthly good, however dear. The grammatical commentators note Mark’s ‘kingdom of God’ for Matthew’s ‘life,’ the two being one; and all agree that it is better to enter that kingdom maimed of the dearest member than, retaining it, to be cast into the fire of hell. The witnesses see in the rising series — hand, foot, eye — a comprehensive demand that nothing whatever, not even the most beloved, be allowed to imperil the soul’s salvation. Barnes refers to the parallel, and Gill that the eye signifies what is ‘dearest to man… the most intimate acquaintance and bosom friends’; the witnesses see in the rising series — hand, foot, eye — a comprehensive demand that nothing whatever, not even the most beloved, be allowed to imperil the soul, it being better to enter the kingdom of God maimed than, keeping the dearest member, to be cast into hell fire.
48:
‘Where their worm dieth not, and the fire is not quenched.’ The few voices note this, the genuine and climactic statement of the refrain, drawn from Isaiah.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes this is the genuine occurrence (unlike the doubtful vv. 44, 46): ‘two bold figures of Gehenna combined (the gnawing worm, the burning flame),’ from Isaiah 66:24 — ‘no figures… can equal the dread reality.’ Gill notes it is repeated to assure the truth and ‘fix an awful impression,’ the Persic rendering it ‘from whence thou shalt never find redemption.’ The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here, on the lamp, is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: this is the climactic and textually secure statement of the dread refrain, sealing the warning about the eye and the whole series. Drawn from the closing verse of Isaiah, it combines the two most terrible images of Gehenna — the worm that never dies and the fire never quenched.

The shared, dominant note is that no figure can equal the reality of which Christ here warns. Having called for the cutting off of hand, foot, and eye, he grounds the demand in the horror of the doom they would otherwise share: a punishment whose worm does not die and whose fire is not quenched, from which, as Gill notes, there is no redemption. The witnesses see in Christ’s threefold repetition of Isaiah’s words his own most solemn sanction of the reality and perpetuity of hell, set deliberately against the disciples’ light ambition to awaken them, and us, to the eternal stakes of the soul’s salvation. Gill notes it is repeated to ‘fix an awful impression,’ the Persic rendering it ‘from whence thou shalt never find redemption’; the witnesses see in Christ’s threefold repetition of Isaiah’s words his most solemn sanction of the reality and perpetuity of hell, set deliberately against the disciples’ light ambition to awaken them, and us, to the eternal stakes of the soul’s salvation. Robertson notes this is the genuine occurrence, unlike the doubtful verses 44 and 46, combining the two boldest figures of Gehenna — the gnawing worm and the burning flame.
49:
‘For every one shall be salted with fire, and every sacrifice shall be salted with salt.’ The witnesses agree this is among the most difficult sayings, and offer differing keys.

The grammatical-historical commentators canvass the senses. Barnes calls it a passage that ‘has given more perplexity… than’ almost any other: the common view is that, as salt preserves, so the fire of hell preserves the wicked in being amid their suffering; another, that the apostles by trials are prepared ‘as a sacrifice’ (Leviticus 2:13). Ellicott and Clarke note the textual difficulties, some manuscripts omitting one clause or the other, and the allusion to Isaiah 66:24. Lightfoot ties ‘every one’ to those ‘whose worm dieth not.’

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes the two clauses: of eternal fire on the reprobate, and of believers who are to ‘offer themselves now to God to be seasoned, that they may be devoted sacrifices,’ and not draw on themselves the fire that is never quenched. Wesley reads it likewise: the unrepentant ‘salted with fire,’ preserved not consumed; the acceptable sacrifice ‘salted’ with divine grace.

The shared, dominant note, amid the acknowledged difficulty, is a contrast between two ‘saltings.’ The wicked shall be ‘salted with fire’ — the unquenchable fire just named preserving them in their torment; while the believer, like the Levitical sacrifice that must be salted, is to be seasoned now with the salt of grace and self-denial, offering himself a living and acceptable sacrifice. The witnesses agree the saying, however obscure in detail, presses the alternative set before every soul: to be ‘salted’ in this life by grace unto God, or hereafter by the fire of his judgment. Lightfoot ties ‘every one’ to those ‘whose worm dieth not,’ and Clarke and Ellicott note the textual difficulties, some manuscripts omitting one clause or the other; the witnesses agree the saying, however obscure in detail, presses the alternative set before every soul — to be ‘salted’ in this life by grace unto God, or hereafter by the fire of his judgment — the believer seasoned now as a living and acceptable sacrifice. Calvin distinguishes the two clauses — of eternal fire on the reprobate, and of believers who offer themselves now to be seasoned as devoted sacrifices (Leviticus 2:13).
50:
‘Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his saltness… Have salt in yourselves, and have peace one with another.’ The witnesses agree this closes the discourse, binding the salt of grace to peace among the disciples.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson recalls that Jesus had called them ‘the salt of the earth’ (Matthew 5:13) and warned against losing the savour — salt that is saltless ‘is of no use.’ Ellicott notes that here the salt is ‘the inward grace… which alone makes the Church what it ought to be,’ and that ‘have peace’ points back to the contention of v.33 — purity from selfish aims being ‘the chief preservative of peace.’ Barnes glosses ‘have salt’ as the ‘preserving, purifying principle’ of self-denial.

The Reformed Calvin connects the salt with the ‘seasoning of good odour’ obtained by faith and the wisdom of the Spirit (compare Colossians 4:6), and the latter clause with the mutual peace it promotes. Wesley paraphrases the obscure passage and bids them ‘retain your savour… and have peace one with another.’

The shared, dominant note is that the disciples are to keep within themselves the salt of grace and self-denial, and so to live at peace. The witnesses tie the saying to the whole discourse: the salt that seasons and preserves is the inward grace of humility and self-mortification, the loss of which makes a disciple, like saltless salt, good for nothing; and the proof and fruit of that salt is peace with one another — the very peace their dispute over greatness had broken. So Christ ends where he began, calling the ambitious Twelve from rivalry to the lowly, peace-making spirit that alone befits his kingdom. Barnes glosses ‘have salt’ as the preserving, purifying principle of self-denial, and Wesley bids them ‘retain your savour… and have peace one with another’; the witnesses tie the saying to the whole discourse, the salt that seasons being the inward grace of humility, the loss of which makes a disciple good for nothing, and its fruit being the peace their dispute over greatness had broken, so Christ ends where he began, calling the ambitious Twelve from rivalry to a lowly, peace-making spirit.
